channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How the $1.8 Billion Trump Fund May Violate Past Practice and Policy
item.description: The fund that could offer payouts to Trump allies who accuse the government of mistreatment is not only highly unusual but also appears to violate the administration’s own policies.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: On Pam Bondi’s first day as attorney general in February 2025, she signed a directive that appeared to prohibit the kind of fund the Trump administration established this week.

item.title: Trump’s Government Moves to Spare an Unhappy Taxpayer Named Trump
item.description: No president has ever used the federal government to advance his own personal interests and those of his family and allies as expansively and openly as Mr. Trump has.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump seems even less inhibited by the rules, written or unwritten, that governed his predecessors.

item.title: Jan. 6 Police Officers Sue to Block Trump’s Payout Fund
item.description: The two officers accused the administration of creating a “slush fund” to reward rioters and groups that committed violence on behalf of President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston for The New York Times
item.media:description: Officer Daniel Hodges of Washington’s Metropolitan Police, left, and Harry Dunn, then an officer with the U.S. Capitol Police, at a congressional hearing in 2022. The two filed suit on Wednesday.

item.title: News of U.S. Indictment Slow to Reach Cubans Waiting for a Breakthrough
item.description: While many Cubans were divided over the legitimacy of the U.S. charging Raul Castro with murder, the hope for developments that might ease their suffering is widespread. “This has to change.”
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole González for The New York Times
item.media:description: A furniture workshop in Havana carries reminders that the Castro family has been part of Cuba’s ruling elite for more than 65 years.

item.title: Justice Dept. Charges Raúl Castro as Trump Escalates Pressure Campaign Against Cuba
item.description: The indictment was an extraordinary escalation of the Trump administration’s multifaceted pressure campaign against Cuba’s Communist government.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer, Frances Robles and David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Ismael Francisco/Associated Press
item.media:description: The indictment of Raúl Castro, the former president of Cuba, comes as the country’s oil supplies for domestic use and power plants has been exhausted.

item.title: Cuba Says the Trump Administration Is Not Negotiating in Good Faith
item.description: Cuba’s ambassador to the United Nations said in an interview that Havana wanted to talk but the Trump administration was creating pretexts for military action.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ernesto Soberón Guzmán, Cuba’s ambassador to the U.N., in New York on Wednesday.

item.title: Nimitz Aircraft Carrier Enters Caribbean as Trump Pressures Cuba
item.description: The carrier arrived in the southern Caribbean on the same day that the Justice Department announced charges against Raul Castro.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Pablo Porciuncula/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The aircraft carrier U.S.S. Nimitz in Guanabara Bay in Rio de Janeiro, earlier this month.

item.title: Musk’s SpaceX Reveals Its Finances for the First Time as It Readies for IPO
item.description: Mr. Musk’s rocket and satellite maker disclosed its financial performance as it prepares to go public in what is set to be one of the largest offerings to date.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: A successful SpaceX offering would bring riches to Wall Street, the company’s employees and, of course, Elon Musk, who is already the world’s richest person.

item.title: To 2026 Graduates Facing a Bleak Job Market, 1991 Grads Have Some Advice
item.description: The New York Times profiled young job seekers in 1991. Today, their own children are entering a work force in flux.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.category: Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey
item.media:credit: via Sharon Dilling; Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times

item.title: Trump’s Coast Guard Address: ‘Good Looking Men’ and Deja Vu
item.description: President Trump’s commencement address was a mixture of jokes and praise for the seafaring life of the Coast Guard graduates. But there were detours into choppy waters.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald J. Trump at the U.S. Coast Guard Academy in New London, Conn., on Wednesday. It was the second time he delivered the commencement address as commander in chief.

item.title: A May-December Romance for 2 Fifth Avenue Hawks
item.description: The last mate of Pale Male, a celebrity hawk, has returned to their Upper East Side address with a new partner, and a baby chick has been seen.
item.dc:creator: James Barron
item.media:credit: via @BirdCentralPark

item.title: The Hard-Line Military Fraternity Running Iran
item.description: Decision making in Iran is guided by a small group of men associated with the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran showing the three supreme leaders, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomenei, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his son, Mojtaba Khamenei.

item.title: China’s Housing Slump Shows Signs of Bottoming Out. We’ve Been Here Before.
item.description: Property prices in Shanghai, in particular, are rebounding, but the national market still faces an enormous overhang — 90 million empty or unfinished apartments.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: The New York Times
item.media:description: Many families in China poured their savings into apartments once seen as a safe path to wealth, only to watch values unravel.

item.title: Days After Hosting Trump, Xi Serves Up Tea and Smiles to Putin
item.description: During a state visit, the Chinese and Russian leaders cast themselves as a stabilizing force in a world thrown into tumult by Washington.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson and Paul Sonne
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Maxim Shemetov
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia and the Chinese leader Xi Jinping inspecting an honor guard at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: Senate G.O.P. Ready to Drop Ballroom Funds From ICE Bill
item.description: Several Republicans had balked at including the money in an immigration enforcement measure that the party plans to push through on a party-line vote.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump gave reporters a tour Tuesday of the White House ballroom construction project.

item.title: Jeff Bezos Praises Trump’s Second Term as ‘More Mature’
item.description: The Amazon founder and owner of The Washington Post also rejected the idea that he has made changes at his companies to gain favor with the president.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson

item.title: Colorado Governor Censured for Commuting Sentence of Election Denier
item.description: The Colorado State Democratic Party, reflecting the anger of rank-and-file Democratic voters, rebuked Gov. Jared Polis on Wednesday over his decision to release Tina Peters from prison.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Michael Ciaglo for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The man is wildly out of touch with the party he effectively leads.” Gov. Jared Polis of Colorado, a Democrat, infuriated the party faithful when he commuted the prison sentence of an election denier.

item.title: Trump’s Praise of Spencer Pratt Could Doom Him in L.A. Mayor’s Race
item.description: Democrats have been trying to portray Mr. Pratt, a former reality TV star, as a MAGA Republican in the mayor’s race. President Trump just gave them a helping hand.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.category: Los Angeles County Federation of Labor
item.media:credit: Maggie Shannon for The New York Times
item.media:description: Spencer Pratt stands in the ruins of his home, destroyed in the Palisades fire in early 2025. He is now a Los Angeles mayoral contender.

item.title: Trump Gets His Payback in Primary Elections, but It Comes at a Cost in Congress
item.description: Republican senators are angry the president is working to unseat their colleagues. But he is also creating more free agents in his own party in Congress willing to defy him.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: After losing his primary race to a Trump-backed challenger, Senator Bill Cassidy, Republican of Louisiana, voted with Democrats in support of a measure to limit the war in Iran.

item.title: Thomas Massie, a Trump Critic, Causes Latest Midterm Fight Over Israel
item.description: In primary races across the country, debates over Israel are taking center stage, dividing Democrats and Republicans alike.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry/Getty Images
item.media:description: “I would have come out sooner, but I had to call my opponent and concede, and it took a while to find Ed Gallrein in Tel Aviv,” Representative Thomas Massie said in his concession speech on Tuesday night, referring to his Republican opponent.

item.title: Mira Nair Finds a New Audience as Mother of Zohran Mamdani
item.description: “I feel like we have given him to the world,” the filmmaker said in a conversation with The New York Times. Her next movie is about one of India’s greatest artists.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:credit: Atul Loke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mira Nair, the acclaimed film director and mother of Zohran Mamdani, in Amritsar, India, in March.

item.title: OpenAI Prepares to File for an I.P.O. in Coming Weeks
item.description: OpenAI would be one of the most highly anticipated potential initial public offerings, in what is set to be a major year of I.P.O.s for Silicon Valley.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Altman, OpenAI’s chief executive. The company is facing challenges from the tech giant Google and an A.I. rival, Anthropic.

item.title: Meta Lays Off 8,000 Employees, as A.I. Casualties Mount
item.description: Employees have signed petitions against being tracked by A.I. and were trying to figure out who had been let go on Wednesday, as the Silicon Valley giant tries to transform into an A.I.-first company.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan, Kalley Huang and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: Meta’s headquarters in Menlo Park, Calif. The company, which renamed itself for the metaverse, is transforming itself for the era of artificial intelligence.

item.title: Nvidia’s Profit Hits $58.3 Billion as A.I. Boom Gathers More Steam
item.description: The chip maker said its profit in its most recent quarter jumped 211 percent from a year earlier thanks to extreme demand from other big technology companies.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jensen Huang, Nvidia’s chief executive, made a bet years ago on computer chips that would be useful for building artificial intelligence.

item.title: Return to the Land, a Whites-Only Community, Is Sued for Discrimination
item.description: Return to the Land, a 160-acre development requiring members be white and heterosexual, is breaking fair housing and civil rights laws, according to a lawsuit.
item.dc:creator: Debra Kamin
item.media:credit: Whitten Sabbatini for The New York Times
item.media:description: The founders of Return to the Land, a whites-only community in Arkansas, say they aren’t breaking any laws. A new lawsuit will test that argument.

item.title: TSA Pushes to Expand Private Security at Airport Checkpoints
item.description: Driven in part by the chaos of the last shutdown, the government is expanding efforts to use private security staff instead of federal employees at more airports.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:credit: Megan Varner/Getty Images
item.media:description: After the last government shutdown caused long lines at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, the Atlanta City Council moved to study a private screening model.

item.title: Thailand Tightens Visa Rules for Tourists From Dozens of Nations, Including U.S.
item.description: The country had loosened entry requirements after the pandemic to attract visitors, but will now limit many travelers to 30 days without a visa.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:credit: Lauren DeCicca for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new visa policy affects citizens of the United States, Canada, France, Germany, Japan, Singapore and other countries.

item.title: He Was Jailed Over a Charlie Kirk Post. The Sheriff Now Owes Him $835,000.
item.description: Larry Bushart sued a Tennessee sheriff who claimed he wanted to incite hysteria with a post after Mr. Kirk’s killing and jailed him for 37 days.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Charity Rachelle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Larry Bushart at his home in Linden, Tenn.

item.title: Trump Just Pardoned Himself and His Family Forever
item.description: By issuing a prohibition on federal investigations that might threaten Trump’s finances, the Justice Department has placed the president and his family in a new category.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Toobin
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Hollywood Ghosted the Cannes Film Festival This Year
item.description: Fewer movie stars, fewer oligarch megayachts in the bay, fewer parties and ever more desperate paparazzi.
item.dc:creator: Sharon Waxman
item.media:credit: Illustration by Sam Whitney/The New York Times; source photographs by adventtr/Getty Images

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Pays His Respects to ‘The Late Show with Stephen Colbert’
item.description: Kimmel said he hoped others will also tune in to CBS “for the last time” to watch Thursday’s “Late Show” finale.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Soccer Game Between North and South Korean Teams Draws Strong Emotion On and Off the Pitch
item.description: While diplomatic relations between the two Koreas are near a historic low, a rare trip by athletes from the North triggered intense emotions in some older South Koreans.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Chung Kyung-jo, center, waving, supported the visiting team in the match between two Korean teams, in Suwon, South Korea on Wednesday.

item.title: 3 Die After Suspected Exposure to Unknown Substance in New Mexico
item.description: More than a dozen emergency officials were hospitalized after responding to a home in a town near Albuquerque. The authorities said there was no threat to the public.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.media:credit: Savannah Peters/Associated Press
item.media:description: Three people have died after being exposed to an unidentified substance in Mountainair, N.M.

item.title: Barney Frank, Gay Pioneer and Liberal Stalwart in Congress, Dies at 86
item.description: Often voted the “brainiest,” “funniest” and “most eloquent” member of the House, he was also the first to come out voluntarily and helped normalize being openly gay in public office.
item.dc:creator: Katharine Q. Seelye
item.category: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010)
